description: Lock Copper Wiring Production to early electrical copper conductors anchored by Faraday-era electromagnetic apparatus, not ancient copper metallurgy or fully mature modern building-wire systems.

checks.reason: The corrected node is anchored to Faraday's 1831 induction and generator work using wire/copper conductors rather than the old Industrial era-default placeholder.
Electrical copper conductors require practical copper working while not claiming every conductor form follows the same production route.
Copper supply depends on mining and metal production as an enabling material base.
Mass production belongs to later commercial scale-up of wire and cable markets, not to Faraday's first-known electrical-conductor evidence.
Steam power could support industrial manufacturing scale, but the early electrical-conductor concept and Faraday apparatus were not steam-engine dependent.
